close_reason: Strategy written with 4 steps (scope, factory, deterministic data, validate parallel). BB Integration Testing step 3 updated to reference this strategy.
description: Create strategy for No Shared Fate. Covers: factory-based test setup (each test gets fresh scoped state), per-test isolation for parallel safety, no shared package-level mutable state in tests, tests that work in imperfect conditions (not requiring pristine DB). Also covers: defaults that match production — test helpers should default to production-realistic configuration, not convenient fictions (e.g., in-memory store when prod uses PostgreSQL). Divergent defaults are a FEAL concern that manifests in test setup. Go guidelines to define underneath: per-test factory pattern, t.Parallel() usage, scoped test identities (unique user/team/document per test), no package-level var in test files holding mutable state. Cross-references: related to 'Compliance Test Suites' (uses factory pattern).
notes: When writing this strategy, reference: Scrub on Entry (bs-61m) for production-realistic defaults vs convenient fictions (test defaults that diverge from prod are a FEAL concern). Coordinator Test Design (bs-owr) for how isolation requirements differ between compute tests (stateless, naturally isolated) and coordinator tests (mock lifecycle).
title: Create strategy: Isolated Test Execution

description: Create strategy for Earned Abstraction. Covers: the rule of three (don't abstract until 3 concrete cases), recognizing the boolean-mode smell as a signal of premature abstraction, refactoring mode-flag abstractions into strategy pattern (named collaborator + shared core/harness), when duplication is preferable to abstraction. Guard candidates: lint for boolean parameters that switch behavior in otherwise shared functions. Cross-references: upstream of 'Coordinator Test Design' (CoC), connects to Consistent Beats Correct (keep duplication over wrong abstraction).
notes: When writing this strategy, reference: Coordinator Test Design (bs-owr) for the boolean-mode → strategy-function refactoring (coordinators vs computation informs when an abstraction is mixing concerns). Test Data Builders (bs-rxe) as a concrete example of earned abstraction (extract builder after 3+ tests need the same setup).
title: Create strategy: Earned Abstraction

close_reason: Strategy written to strategies/scrub-on-entry.md. Reviewed, red-teamed, all quality checks pass.
description: Strategy for Fail Early and Loud. Validate everything at system boundaries before it enters domain logic. The refined Postel's Law: be tolerant of additions you don't use (unknown fields are safe to ignore — this lets upstream systems grow per Grow Don't Break), strict about values you depend on (an enum you don't recognize in a field you act on is unsafe). If your code branches on it, computes with it, or stores it as a fact, validate it. DDD anti-corruption layer concept: the system interior works only with pristine objects that have passed all checks. Uncle Bob's clean architecture boundary: tight core, validated inputs. The biological metaphor: like surgical scrubbing, you clean at the boundary to protect what's inside.
notes: FOUNDATION STRATEGY: downstream strategies reference the boundary validation pattern defined here. Higher coherence bar — vocabulary established here propagates everywhere. When writing this strategy, reference: Coordinator Test Design (bs-owr) for how validation relates to the compute/coordinate split (validation is computation at the boundary, called by a coordinator). Domain-First Packaging (bs-u06) for where boundaries live in the package structure. Grow Don't Break (bs-uzp) for the tolerant reader principle (accept unknown additions, reject unknown values you branch on).
title: Create strategy: Scrub on Entry

description:
Epic to validate whether the principle/strategy/guidelines/guard layers each add meaningful value. Test task: build a Fastmail JMAP email-to-Atom-feed service.

## Test Task Description

Build a service that:
1. Connects to Fastmail via JMAP
2. Lists all mailboxes, displays them nested with checkboxes in <ol> elements
3. Saves checkbox state; on first save for a folder, fetches all unread emails
4. Creates folder structure in output dir mirroring selected mailboxes
5. Creates initial-archive.xml (Atom feed with all unread) and feed.xml (empty, with prev-archive pointing to initial-archive.xml)

### Features
- **refresh**: Opens selected folders, finds new emails since last check, appends to feed.xml
- **prune**: Moves items older than 1 week from feed.xml into archive-<ISO year>w<ISO week>.xml. New archive gets prev-archive pointing to feed.xml current prev-archive. feed.xml prev-archive updates to point to the new archive file. feed.xml always points to the current week.

### Testing Requirements
- E2E tests with Playwright that verify the full flow works
- E2E tests MUST NOT hit Fastmail — use captured/mocked JMAP responses (do not prescribe a specific mocking tool; let the agent choose its approach)
- Unit tests for the core logic (feed construction, archive chain management, etc.)

### Success Criteria
- Well-tested, working code
- Follows design and testing principles (where applicable per test condition)
- Minimal handholding needed

## Experiment Design
Run the test task under different conditions and compare:
- How much intervention/correction was needed
- Quality of architecture and abstractions
- Test quality (behavior-driven vs coverage theater)
- Correctness of Atom feed structure and archive chain logic
- What testing approach the agent chose for API mocking (signal for design judgment)
- Overall code quality
title: Validate bjornstack layers

description: Define what a strategy is within bjornstack. This is the foundational task — all other workflow decisions depend on it. Must produce: (1) Document how the four layers relate through the WHY/HOW/WHAT frame: Principle=WHY (why it matters), Strategy=HOW (the abstract approach), Guideline=WHAT (concrete language-specific steps), Guard=enforcement of WHAT. This framing should be part of README.md and any format docs. (2) A format spec for strategy files (like FORMAT.md for principles). What fields? What level of detail? What makes a good vs bad strategy? (3) Clear definition of the boundary between strategy and guideline — when does something belong in the strategy vs in a language-specific guideline? (4) When is a strategy needed vs when a principle is direct enough to skip straight to guidelines? (5) Define the divergence documentation format — how to record when a language can't follow part of a strategy. (6) Update README.md to reflect the four-layer system with WHY/HOW/WHAT framing. (7) Create a FORMAT equivalent for strategies.
title: Define strategy as a concept

description: Strategy primarily for Many More Much Smaller Steps, also serves Don't Fly Blind and No Shared Fate. Ship behind feature flags to merge domain design early and limit blast radius (50/50 motivation). Key points: flags default to OFF — code is wired but inert until explicitly enabled. Flag lifecycle: rollout flags (temporary, plan cleanup before shipping) vs targeting flags (long-lived, first-class config for different deploy targets or customer segments). Walking skeletons behind flags let the team build on new domain design immediately. Rollback becomes 'flip a flag' with granular targeting (specific users, 1% → 5% → 100%). Note FEAL interaction: a flag defaulting to OFF is an explicit design choice, not a silent path — no FEAL violation. Cross-cutting: serves MMMSS (early integration), DFB (canary-style rollout gives signal), NSF (isolates blast radius).
notes: When writing this strategy, reference: Grow Don't Break (bs-uzp) for when flags serve as the migration mechanism for breaking changes. Service Boundary Communication (bs-v9e) for where flags live in a multi-service architecture. Tidy or Change (bs-p73) for how flags enable shipping structural changes separately from behavioral ones.
title: Create strategy: Flag Your Changes

description: Go-specific guideline implementing the Tidy or Change strategy for Many More Much Smaller Steps. title: Create Go guideline for Tidy or Change

description: Create a new skill at .claude/skills/guidelines-editor/SKILL.md. The skill: (1) Reads the source principle and its strategy. Refuses to proceed if no strategy exists — prompts user to create one first. (2) Targets a specific language. (3) Walks through the strategy point by point, deriving concrete language-specific guidelines. (4) For each strategy point, checks: can this be followed as-is in this language? If not, documents the divergence and why in implementation/<lang>/<name>/divergences.md. (5) For each guideline, assesses guard candidacy ('Can this be mechanically checked with zero judgment?'). (6) Writes guidelines to implementation/<lang>/<name>/guidelines.md, including a 'Guard candidates' section. (7) Creates a bead for each guard candidate. (8) For judgment-only or not-applicable principles, writes a file explaining why.
title: Create guidelines-editor skill

close_reason: Strategy-editor skill created at .claude/skills/strategy-editor/SKILL.md with all 20 improvements from iterative review
description:
Create a new skill at .claude/skills/strategy-editor/SKILL.md. Three modes (mirroring principle-editor): Create, Review, Extract.

Create mode: (1) User describes a technique or provides a principle name. (2) Draft the strategy in strategies/FORMAT.md format. (3) Run quality checks: name is 2-4 word noun phrase, PRINCIPLE link references a real principle, steps are numbered/abstract/language-agnostic, OUTCOMES are observable, MISAPPLICATION is realistic, SKIP WHEN is present. (4) Layer check: not a principle (needs multiple steps), not a guideline (no language references), not a tutorial. (5) Coherence check: steps follow from principle, outcomes satisfy the principle, misapplication is realistic, skip-when is honest. (6) Write to strategies/<kebab-name>.md. (7) Offer handoff to guidelines-editor.

Review mode: Evaluate a draft strategy against all quality checks. Show concrete rewrites for failures.

Extract mode: Given prose docs or best practices, identify strategies vs. other layers. Produce formatted strategies.

Reference: docs/strategy-creation-workflow.md for full workflow context.
title: Create strategy-editor skill

close_reason: Strategy written to strategies/tidy-or-change.md
description: Strategy for Many More Much Smaller Steps. Kent Beck's Tidy First? framing: structural and behavioral changes go in separate changesets. Choose the order based on context — tidy first to make the change easy, or make the change then tidy. The constraint: never both in the same changeset. In a structural PR, any behavioral diff is a bug. In a behavioral PR, any structural diff is noise. Mixing them makes review unreliable. Lineage: Kent Beck, Tidy First?
notes: Largely standalone. When writing this strategy, reference: Flag Your Changes (bs-jgv) for how flags enable the tidy-then-change workflow by letting you ship structural changes behind an inert flag.
title: Create strategy: Tidy or Change

Go guidelines to define underneath: a.* builder conventions in test/a/ package, options pattern for SUT, Build() returns value not pointer, static UUIDs for reproducibility. Cross-references: related to 'Whole-Object Assertion' and 'Parameterized Test Structure'.
notes: When writing this strategy, reference: Isolated Test Execution (bs-033) for the factory concept that builders implement. Earned Abstraction (bs-5ht) for when to extract test helpers into builders (rule of three). Whole-Object Assertion (bs-8bd) for how builders create expected values for comparison.

close_reason: Strategy written to strategies/domain-first-packaging.md. 6 steps, 5 outcomes, rationale covering foundation status, domain identification, boundary types, dependency direction, and BYN relationship.
description: Strategy for Structure Is Intent + Be Your Name. Addresses package organization: naming, type placement, dependency direction, library wrapping. Absorbs Boundary Type Placement. Also covers domain-driven package structure (folded from bs-4tw): organizing code by domain (cart/, pricing/, notifications/) not by technical role (models/, controllers/, services/), package boundaries that constrain where code belongs, eliminating catch-all packages. Also covers namespace-aware naming (folded from bs-n5q): smurf naming avoidance (mail.Mail, user.User) where the exported name stutters against the package name — in Go and Python where the package is always part of the qualified name, the export name should add information not repeat it. These concerns weren't strong enough to stand alone but are important and should be addressed in this strategy. Go guidelines to define underneath: package naming conventions, no utils/helpers/common packages, internal/ usage patterns, smurf-naming avoidance rules. Guard candidates: lint for banned package names (utils, helpers, common, misc), lint for exported type names that match package name.
notes: FOUNDATION STRATEGY: downstream strategies reference the packaging decisions defined here. Higher coherence bar — vocabulary established here propagates everywhere. When writing this strategy, reference: Coordinator Test Design (bs-owr) for how compute/coordinate classification maps to package boundaries. Scrub on Entry (bs-61m) for validation at package/service boundaries. Intentionally Public (existing strategy) for export decisions within the packaging model.
title: Create strategy: Domain-First Packaging

close_reason: Strategy written with internal/external asymmetry, wire-contract-holds technique, security carve-out, and batching economics
description: Strategy for No Shared Fate. Only additive changes to public/external APIs — new endpoints, new fields, new capabilities. Never remove, rename, or go back on a previously stated fact. When breaking changes are genuinely necessary, use semantic versioning (major = breaking) so consumers opt in explicitly. Help consumers upgrade when a major version bump is needed. Rich Hickey's accretion vs breakage framing: a system that grows is one others can depend on; a system that breaks teaches consumers to fear upgrades. Note distinction from Intentionally Public strategy (SII): GDB governs external API contracts at service boundaries; IP governs code-level export decisions within a package.
notes: When writing this strategy, reference: Scrub on Entry (bs-61m) for the tolerant reader pattern (be tolerant of additions, strict on values you branch on — this is the consumer-side of GDB). Service Boundary Communication (bs-v9e) for what contracts exist at boundaries. Flag Your Changes (bs-jgv) for how feature flags serve as the mechanism when breaking changes are genuinely necessary.
title: Create strategy: Grow Don't Break

close_reason: Strategy written to strategies/boundary-communication.md. Renamed from Service Boundary Communication to Boundary Communication — scope broadened to cover packages, modules, and services.
description: Create strategy for No Shared Fate and Structure Is Intent. Covers: services communicate through APIs or messages, not shared databases. Each service owns its data and exposes it through explicit interfaces. No reaching into another service's tables. Architecture-level — guidelines are per-stack. Hard line: if two services need the same data, one owns it and the other requests it.
notes: When writing this strategy, reference: Domain-First Packaging (bs-u06) for where service boundaries map to package structure. Scrub on Entry (bs-61m) for how services validate incoming data at boundaries. Grow Don't Break (bs-uzp) for how service APIs evolve without breaking consumers.
title: Create strategy: Boundary Communication

close_reason: Closed
description: Create strategies/testing__contract.md — the contract testing strategy. Covers: consumer-side (encode expectations of external party behavior) vs provider-side (validate our integration per consumer contracts) fork. Parent meta-strategy: Test Layer Selection (strategies/testing.md).
title: Create strategy: Contract Testing

description: Projects need a one-time decision about which test layers (unit, BB integration, contract, e2e) they adopt and how each layer is executed. Currently the meta-strategy (Test Layer Selection) presents all layers, but not all apply to every project — showing unused layers is noise. This is also where operational decisions like 'run consumer contracts against staging daily' live. One concrete technique to evaluate: when an external party hasn't adopted CDC, running consumer contracts against their staging/dev environment on a schedule (e.g. daily) can bridge the gap. It's not how CDC is supposed to work, but for less-trafficked flows where production monitoring alone might miss a regression, a coarse daily signal beats no signal. This bead should be addressed after all test sub-strategies are written, when the full set of layer options is visible.
title: Decide how projects configure which test layers and strategies to use

description: Run the test task (see bs-9qz) with principles AND language-specific guidelines loaded. No strategies or guards. Track handholding needed and output quality.
title: Principles + guidelines: email-to-atom service

description: Cross-strategy cohesiveness review found missing cross-references: (1) DFP should reference EA's PATTERNS.md for cross-domain infrastructure patterns. (2) Boundary Communication should reference Contract Testing as verification mechanism for service-level contracts, and vice versa. (3) Unit Testing should reference Isolated Test Execution. (4) Unit Testing should mention EA's configuration bloat check as a related detection signal.
title: Add cross-references: DFP↔EA, BC↔Contract Testing, UT↔ITE, UT↔EA config bloat
